title: Timing In stand-up Comedy: Text, Audio, Laughter, Kinesics (TIC-TALK): Pipeline and Database for the Multimodal Study of Comedic Timing
venue: Proceedings of the 2nd Workshop on Computational Humor (CHum 2026), pages 29–38
research_questions: How can comedic timing in stand-up be represented computationally as short-lag coordination across text, gesture, and audience response?
Can a modular pipeline align subtitles, detected laughter, shot composition, and pose-derived motion at their native temporal resolutions?
Do thematic content, kinematic profile, and shot composition co-vary with audience laughter across topic segments?
Given the multimodal stream up to time t, can a model predict whether a new laughter event will begin in [t, t+2)?

datasets_used.name: TIC-TALK
datasets_used.size: 5,416 topic segments across 90 shows; average runtime per show is 63 min (total ≈94 h); 322,973 frames at 1 fps; 22% are full-body frames

datasets_used.name: Manual shot-classification frame annotations
datasets_used.size: 594 manually annotated frames and 128 held-out samples

datasets_used.name: TIC-TALK short-horizon laughter onset prediction anchors
datasets_used.size: 285,916 anchors across 90 shows; positive rate 17.0%; test set: 45,894 anchors from 14 held-out shows

methods_proposed.name: TIC-TALK multimodal processing pipeline
methods_proposed.summary: A pipeline that processes subtitles, audio, and video from stand-up specials into temporally aligned topic, laughter, shot, and pose streams. It uses BERTopic and sentence-BERT for text, Whisper-AT for laughter events, YOLOv8-cls for shot type, and YOLOv8s-pose for performer keypoints.

methods_proposed.name: Hierarchical temporal alignment without resampling
methods_proposed.summary: Topic segments serve as 60 s anchors, and higher-frequency pose keyframes, shot labels, and laughter events are assigned by temporal containment. Each modality keeps its native resolution rather than being resampled.

methods_proposed.name: Pose-derived kinematic signals
methods_proposed.summary: The paper defines arm spread A_t, kinetic energy E_t, and trunk lean θ_t from raw 17-joint pose coordinates. These continuous signals are smoothed with a 30 s sliding window and used as proxies for performance dynamics.

methods_proposed.name: Short-horizon laughter onset prediction benchmark
methods_proposed.summary: Given multimodal context up to time t, the task predicts whether a new laughter event will begin in [t, t+2). The benchmark samples 285,916 anchors at 1 s steps and evaluates feature ablations for history, text, and vision with show-level train/validation/test splits.

humor_theories.detail: The introduction cites Kant’s definition of laughter as arising from “the sudden transformation of a strained expectation into nothing” to motivate attention to timing and expectation.

The paper cites Bergson’s “something mechanical encrusted on the living” and Augustine’s difficulty of defining time to motivate embodiment, rupture, and timing; it states that it did not test a pre-formulated theory of which techniques elicit the most laughter.

evaluation_methods.detail: Shot classification was validated on 128 held-out annotated samples and reported average F1 = 0.91.
Topic model selection used number of topics K, largest-topic share s_max, normalized topic entropy H_norm, NPMI coherence C_NPMI, and the composite score S = H_norm + C_NPMI - 2 s_max.
Audience response was operationalized through detected laughter in recorded stand-up specials; laughter rate r_l was defined as the share of block duration covered by detected laughter events.
The cross-modal use case computed topic-level means and Pearson correlations between laughter rate and kinematic or shot-composition features across 24 topics.
The laughter onset benchmark used AUROC, AUPRC, F1, precision, and recall; AUPRC of a random classifier equaled the positive rate, 0.170.

headline_results.finding: The fine-tuned YOLOv8-cls shot classifier achieved high validation performance on held-out annotated frames.
Kinetic energy was the strongest non-tautological cross-modal signal and negatively predicted topic-level laughter rate.
The highest-laughter topics were personal and bodily, while geopolitical or identity-framing topics produced lower laughter rates.
Belly laughs were nearly absent at 60 s topic granularity.
headline_results.value: T22 = 0.0051; T17 = 0.0061; fewer than 2 events per 74,000+ inference windows

headline_results.finding: Close-up shot proportion had a weak positive relationship with topic-level laughter rate.
The best laughter onset predictor was the full multimodal system, but it only marginally improved over laughter history alone.
headline_results.dataset: TIC-TALK short-horizon laughter onset prediction anchors

key_findings: TIC-TALK provides derived, temporally aligned annotations for 90 professionally edited Netflix stand-up specials, including topic segments, detected laughter events, shot labels, pose keypoints, and sentence-BERT embeddings.
Across 24 topics, mean kinetic energy correlated negatively with laughter rate (r = -0.75, N = 24), supporting a correlational stillness-before-punchline interpretation.
Personal and bodily topics had the highest laughter rates, while geopolitical and identity-framing topics generated substantially less laughter in the corpus-level analysis.
Belly laughs were almost invisible at 60 s topic granularity, motivating finer event-level annotation.
For short-horizon laughter onset prediction, laughter history dominated: the full text + vision + history model improved AUROC by only 0.004 over history-only.
Text and vision showed small multimodal synergy, with text + vision AUROC = 0.577 compared with text-only 0.554 and vision-only 0.538.
limitations: The corpus is limited to Netflix stand-up comedy specials and does not represent stand-up comedy in its totality.
Netflix platform selection is itself a marker of symbolic recognition and may reflect career, curation, and industrial production criteria not modeled in the paper.
Quantitative and computational analysis cannot fully account for comic intent, artistic singularity, comic intuition, or poetic inclination.
Topic modeling captures broad themes but does not operationalize the internal structure of jokes, such as setups, punchlines, or callbacks.
Professionally filmed stand-up specials are not neutral records of stage performance; montage and camera choices are part of the object under study and can confound visual signals.
The E_t–laughter anti-correlation is correlational and may be confounded by filming conventions, performer mobility, and the artefactual T6 topic.
The 60 s granularity of topical context limits short-horizon prediction, and the paper suggests sentence-level and frame-level features would likely yield stronger gains.
Single-recording specials and stochastic audience response limit the ceiling of laughter onset prediction.
safety_ethics_notes: The released corpus contains only derived, time-aligned annotations; no audio, image, or video is distributed. The paper also notes Netflix/platform selection and filming conventions as embedded biases, and states that Claude AI was used to assist with condensing and proofreading the paper.

artifacts.other: Derived annotations are serialized as hierarchical JSON per show; visualizations for all 90 specials are distributed with the corpus.

human_samples.population: Audiences audible in 90 professionally filmed Netflix stand-up comedy specials from 2015–2024.
human_samples.recruitment: Not recruited by the researchers; audience response was taken from archival/platform comedy-special recordings.
human_samples.observations: Audience response was operationalized through automatically detected laughter events; the paper reports mean laughter coverage of 17.8%, about 1.2 events per 10 s, and a 17.0% positive rate for 2 s laughter-onset anchors.
human_samples.detail: No audience participant counts, demographics, venue details, or per-show audience sizes are reported.

human_samples.population: Stand-up performers/comedians appearing in the 90 Netflix stand-up comedy specials.
human_samples.recruitment: Not recruited by the researchers; performances were selected as Netflix stand-up specials.
human_samples.observations: 90 stand-up performances with average runtime 63 min, total approximately 94 h.
human_samples.detail: The paper describes a corpus of 90 professionally edited Netflix specials but does not report the number of distinct comedians/performers or their demographics.

human_samples.population: Manual frame annotators for shot classification.
human_samples.observations: 594 manually annotated training frames and 128 held-out annotated validation samples for six shot-type labels.
human_samples.detail: The paper does not report who annotated the frames, annotator count, expertise, instructions, adjudication, or inter-annotator agreement.

judges.name: Recorded stand-up audiences
judges.detail: Audience laughter is the target response signal for descriptive laughter-rate analyses and the laughter-onset prediction benchmark, although the events are identified by an automatic audio tagger rather than manual audience coding.

judges.name: Whisper-AT
judges.detail: Pretrained AudioSet-based audio tagging model used at 0.8 s stride in a high-recall configuration to label laughter types, confidence scores, and event start/end times; these labels determine laughter rates and prediction targets.

judges.name: Manual shot-frame annotators
judges.detail: Provided shot-type labels for 594 training frames and 128 held-out validation samples; expertise and blinding are not reported.

judges.name: YOLOv8-cls
judges.detail: Fine-tuned shot classifier used to assign one of six shot labels at 1 Hz; validation against held-out human annotations yielded average F1 = 0.91.

judges.name: YOLOv8s-pose
judges.detail: Pretrained pose estimator used on filtered full-body/frontal frames to extract 17 COCO body keypoints at 1 fps; derived arm spread, kinetic energy, and trunk lean are computed from these outputs.

judges.name: BERTopic with all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embeddings, UMAP, and HDBSCAN
judges.detail: Used to assign topic labels to 60 s text blocks and to provide 384-dimensional sentence-BERT embeddings stored in each topic segment.

judges.name: NPMI coherence and composite topic-selection score
judges.detail: Training block size for topic modeling was selected from 120, 150, 180, 210, and 240 s using K, largest-topic share, normalized entropy, NPMI coherence, and S = H_norm + C_NPMI - 2 s_max with validity constraints.

judges.name: Pearson correlations and topic-level weighted means
judges.detail: Used for the cross-modal use case relating topic-level laughter rate to kinetic energy, arm spread, trunk lean, and shot proportions across 24 BERTopic topics.

judges.name: AUROC, AUPRC, F1, precision, recall
judges.detail: Used to evaluate the short-horizon laughter-onset prediction systems; F1/precision/recall use a validation-tuned decision threshold.

generation_selection.detail: The paper does not run a humor/text generation experiment and does not select among multiple generated outputs; it builds a multimodal dataset and evaluates automatic labeling, descriptive analyses, and a classifier benchmark.

model_provenance.name: Whisper-AT
model_provenance.detail: Described as a pretrained AudioSet-based audio tagging model; no checkpoint, software version, inference date, or access mode is reported.

model_provenance.name: all-MiniLM-L6-v2
model_provenance.detail: Sentence-transformer encoder used for 384-dimensional block embeddings; no package version, checkpoint hash, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: BERTopic
model_provenance.detail: Used with UMAP and HDBSCAN for topic modeling; algorithmic hyperparameters are reported, but software versions/snapshots are not.

model_provenance.name: YOLOv8-cls
model_provenance.detail: Fine-tuned for six-way shot classification on 594 annotated frames and validated on 128 held-out samples; exact YOLOv8 checkpoint/package version is not reported.

model_provenance.name: YOLOv8s-pose
model_provenance.detail: Pretrained pose model used for 17-joint keypoint extraction; exact checkpoint/package version and inference date are not reported.

model_provenance.name: HistGradientBoostingClassifier
model_provenance.detail: Classifier used for laughter-onset prediction with balanced sample weights; software/library version and random seed are not reported.

model_provenance.name: Claude AI
model_provenance.detail: Acknowledged as assisting with condensing and proofreading the paper; not used as an evaluator or to produce empirical results. Exact Claude model/version is not reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Inference at 0.8 s stride over audio.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Paper says a high-recall configuration was used and contiguous positive windows were merged into continuous events; thresholds and other decoding/configuration details are not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Each event stores start/end time, laughter type label, and confidence score.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Subtitle lines are concatenated into time blocks; candidate training block sizes are 120, 150, 180, 210, and 240 s; final topic assignment is to 60 s blocks.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: UMAP: cosine distance, n_neighbors=15, n_components=5, min_dist=0.1. HDBSCAN: min_cluster_size=15, min_samples=5. Topic model capped at 40 topics; top 10 words retained. Outlier reassignment uses embedding/c-TF-IDF reduction, nearest normalized centroid with similarity at least 0.30, and within-show temporal gap filling.
inference_budgets.detail: Embeddings are computed in batches and L2-normalized, but batch size, hardware, runtime, and cost are not reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: One shot label per video frame sampled at 1 Hz.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Fine-tuning used 100 epochs, batch size 32, learning rate 1e-3, and six shot classes: full shot, medium close-up, medium long shot, medium shot, other angles, and other.
inference_budgets.detail: Training used 594 manually annotated frames; validation used 128 held-out samples.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: YOLOv8s-pose is applied at 1 fps to filtered full-body/frontal frames only.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: 17 COCO keypoints are stored; joints with no detection confidence are recorded as (0, 0) and excluded by a validity filter; arm spread, kinetic energy, and trunk lean are smoothed with a 30 s sliding window.
inference_budgets.detail: The paper reports 322,973 visual frames at 1 fps, with 22% full-body frames yielding raw keypoint sequences for pose analysis.

inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: For each of 5,416 aligned blocks, laughter rate, per-frame kinematic features, and shot-type proportions are computed; topic-level profiles are averaged weighted by block count; Pearson correlations are computed across 24 topics.
inference_budgets.detail: This is an analysis aggregation rather than generative inference.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Anchor points sampled at 1 s steps, excluding moments inside an ongoing laughter event; 285,916 anchors total across 90 shows; test set has 45,894 anchors from 14 held-out shows.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Prediction target is whether a new laughter event begins in [t, t+2). GroupShuffleSplit by show: 62 train, 14 validation, 14 test. History and vision features aggregate the past 10 s; text uses the current topic block's 384-dimensional embedding reduced to 64 dimensions by PCA fitted on the training set only. HistGradientBoostingClassifier uses balanced sample weights; threshold tuned on validation to maximize F1.
inference_budgets.detail: Ablations evaluate history-only, text-only, vision-only, text+vision, and text+vision+history.
The paper reports only that Claude AI assisted with condensing and proofreading; no prompt, token, date, version, or cost information is provided.

human_baselines.description: The benchmark includes a random AUPRC baseline equal to the positive rate, but no human prediction baseline.
human_baselines.detail: No humans were asked to predict laughter onsets from matched multimodal context.

human_baselines.scope: Shot classification, topic labeling, laughter detection, and cross-modal analyses
human_baselines.description: Human annotations are used only as shot-classification training/validation labels; they are not a human baseline system compared against model predictions under matched conditions.
human_baselines.detail: No human baseline is reported for detecting laughter, assigning topics, extracting pose, or estimating the cross-modal relationships.

mitigations: The TIC-TALK derived dataset and laughter-onset benchmark are newly introduced rather than a legacy public benchmark evaluated by later models.
The laughter-onset benchmark uses show-level train/validation/test splitting, so no show appears in more than one split.
PCA for text features is fitted on the training set only.
Shot-classifier validation uses held-out annotated samples separate from the 594 annotated training frames.
contamination_risk.basis: The main evaluated classifier is trained and tested on newly derived anchors with show-level separation, and no proprietary LLM or generative model is evaluated on a public legacy humor benchmark. The source Netflix specials themselves were public/platform media from 2015–2024 and pretrained components may have unknown exposure to related audio/video/text, but the paper's headline supervised benchmark labels and splits are newly constructed. The paper discusses corpus and platform biases, but not data contamination or decontamination tests.

llm_judge_dependence.detail: No LLM is used to judge humor, label outputs, select generations, or compute evaluation metrics. Claude AI is acknowledged only for condensing and proofreading the paper.

reporting_gaps: Audience participant counts, demographics, geography, and per-show audience sizes are not reported.
Number of distinct comedians/performers and their demographics are not reported.
Selection criteria for the 90 Netflix specials are only broadly described; detailed show list and sampling procedure are not given in the paper text.
Manual shot-annotation personnel, annotator count, expertise, instructions, blinding, adjudication, and inter-annotator agreement are not reported.
No human validation is reported for Whisper-AT laughter-event labels, laughter-type labels, pose keypoints, or BERTopic topic assignments.

Exact model checkpoints, package versions, random seeds, access mode, inference dates, and hardware are not reported for Whisper-AT, all-MiniLM-L6-v2, BERTopic/UMAP/HDBSCAN, YOLOv8-cls, YOLOv8s-pose, or HistGradientBoostingClassifier.
The Whisper-AT high-recall configuration is not specified in enough detail to reproduce thresholds or class-decision rules.
Compute time, hardware, and monetary cost are not reported.
The GroupShuffleSplit random seed or exact show IDs in each train/validation/test split are not reported.
Claude AI version, prompts, date of use, and access mode for proofreading/condensing are not reported.
